title: 5.2.1 Graph Traversal and Applications
subtitle: Traversal goals, scenarios, and extension-ready visualization/code/practice

content.introduction: Graph traversal provides systematic node exploration patterns that support reachability, connectivity, and search tasks.

sections.title: 5.2.1.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define traversal goals and key differences between depth-first and breadth-first exploration strategies.
examples: Contrast DFS path focus with BFS level focus

sections.title: 5.2.1.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe generic traversal workflow including visited tracking, frontier management, and disconnected-graph handling.
examples: Traverse a graph represented by adjacency list
Maintain visited state to avoid repetition
Cover disconnected components with repeated starts

sections.title: 5.2.1.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: Discuss complexity O(V+E), representation impacts, and practical criteria for choosing DFS or BFS.
examples: Estimate traversal cost by number of edges
Use BFS for shortest hops, DFS for structure analysis
Pick storage to match sparsity and workload

keyTakeaways: Understand DFS and BFS at conceptual level
Implement traversal control with visited sets
Analyze traversal complexity in common representations
